title: The Nibelungenlied
description: The Nibelungenlied — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: Alberich
memes.description: Alberich is the dwarf guardian of the Nibelung treasure in the Nibelungenlied — a "wild dwarf" of extraordinary strength who, after being defeated by Siegfried, swears fealty and becomes the loyal keeper of the hoard, bridging the boundary between individual character and archetypal figure.

memes.label: Artifacts and Magical Objects in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: A comprehensive survey of the five major artifacts and magical objects in the Nibelungenlied — Balmung, Tarnkappe, the Nibelung Treasure, and the horses and weapons that shape the epic's action.

memes.label: Balmung
memes.description: Balmung is Siegfried's legendary sword in the Nibelungenlied, won from the Nibelung hoard alongside the Tarnkappe — a weapon of extraordinary sharpness that Siegfried wields in battle and that Hagen later uses to execute his final defiance.

memes.label: Beauty and Courtly Appearance in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Schönheit (beauty) and courtly appearance as instruments of social power in the Nibelungenlied — physical beauty determines status, attracts desire, and becomes a weapon wielded by both women and men in the poem's courtly world, where appearance is never merely ornamental but always consequential.

memes.label: Bechelaren
memes.description: Bechelaren is the margraviate of Rüdiger on the Danube in the Nibelungenlied, the crucial waystation on the Burgundians' journey to Etzel's court — the last place of warmth and hospitality before the final catastrophe, and the tragic site of Rüdiger's impossible moral choice.

memes.label: Betrayal and Deception in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Betrayal (Verrat, Untreue) and deception (List) as the narrative engine of the Nibelungenlied — from Siegfried's disguised conquest of Brünhild to Hagen's murderous ambush and Kriemhild's fatal hospitality.

memes.label: Bloedel
memes.description: Bloedel is the brother of King Etzel (Attila) of the Huns — a proud warrior who attacks the Burgundian retinue at the banquet, is killed by Dankwart, and whose death triggers the epic's climactic general battle at the Hunnish court.

memes.label: Blood and Wounds in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Blood (Blut) and wounds (Wunden) as recurring images of violence, vulnerability, and tragic truth-telling in the Nibelungenlied — from Siegfried's dragon-blood bath to the bleeding corpse that accuses Hagen.

memes.label: Boldness and Daring in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Boldness (Kühnheit) as a defining heroic virtue in the Nibelungenlied — the quality that distinguishes Siegfried and the poem's greatest warriors, yet also the force that drives them toward fatal confrontation.

memes.label: Bridal Night Deception
memes.description: The bridal night deception in which Siegfried, invisible under the Tarnkappe, subdues the warrior-queen Brunhild on Gunther's behalf — the second and more intimate deception that deepens the original sin of the bridal quest and provides the physical proof that will later trigger the queens' quarrel.

memes.label: Bridal Quest to Isenstein
memes.description: The bridal quest to Isenstein is Gunther's expedition to win the warrior-queen Brunhild — a journey that requires Siegfried's secret intervention under the Tarnkappe to overcome Brunhild's superhuman strength, establishing the foundational deception that will ultimately destroy the Burgundian court.

memes.label: Brunhild
memes.description: Brunhild is the queen of Isenstein in the Nibelungenlied — a woman of superhuman strength who sets deadly contests for her suitors, is won by Gunther through Siegfried's secret aid, and whose public humiliation in the quarrel of the queens triggers the chain of events leading to Siegfried's murder.

memes.label: Burgundian Court
memes.description: The Burgundian Court is the royal court of the Burgundian kingdom in the Nibelungenlied — centered at Worms on the Rhine, ruled by King Gunther with his brothers Gernot and Geiselher, it is the primary political organization of the epic's first part and the seat from which all tragedy unfolds.

memes.label: Characters of the Burgundian Court
memes.description: A comprehensive listing of all characters associated with the Burgundian Court in the Nibelungenlied, organized by role and rank.

memes.label: Burgundian March
memes.description: The Burgundian March is the border territory of the Burgundian kingdom, the northern frontier where Siegfried leads the Burgundian army to repel the Saxon and Danish invasion in Adventure 4 — the site of the epic's largest pitched battle.

memes.label: Church and Religion in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: The role of the Church, Christian ritual, and religious framework in the Nibelungenlied — from the Münster scenes that structure courtly life to the poem's ambiguous relationship between heroic fate and divine will.

memes.label: Clothing and Textiles in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Clothing (Kleid, Gewand) and textiles — especially silk (Seide) — as markers of status, instruments of diplomacy, and narrative devices in the Nibelungenlied, where what characters wear is inseparable from who they are.

memes.label: Combat and Warfare in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Combat (Kampf, Streit) as the central mode of masculine action in the Nibelungenlied — from single combat and tournament to full-scale military campaign and the apocalyptic hall-battle that consumes the Burgundian court.

memes.label: Counsel and Advice in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Rat (counsel, advice) as the deliberative mechanism of the Nibelungenlied — the formal and informal processes through which decisions are made, alliances are forged, and kings are advised, whose systematic failure drives the poem's catastrophe.

memes.label: Courage and High Spirits in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Courage (hoher Mut, Kühnheit) as the foundational heroic virtue in the Nibelungenlied — the quality that distinguishes the warrior elite, propels Siegfried's exploits, and is tested to destruction in the poem's tragic final act.

memes.label: Courtly Breeding and Discipline in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Courtly breeding and discipline (höfsche Zucht) as the ethical code of the Nibelungenlied's aristocratic world — the system of manners, self-restraint, and social performance that defines courtly identity and whose violation drives the poem's tragedy.

memes.label: Crown and Kingship in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: The crown (Krone) as the central symbol of royal authority in the Nibelungenlied — an object invested with the power to legitimize rule, transfer sovereignty, and mark the boundary between rightful kingship and contested power.

memes.label: Cunning and Guile in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Cunning (List) as a strategic virtue and moral problem in the Nibelungenlied — the art of achieving ends through deception rather than open force, wielded by heroes and villains alike.

memes.label: Custom and Tradition in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Sitte (custom, tradition, proper conduct) as the unwritten law of the Nibelungenlied's courtly world — from Kriemhild's "höfsche Sitte" that is a model for all women to the customs of hospitality, gift-giving, and burial that structure social life, the poem shows a society governed by inherited practice rather than codified law, a world in which to violate custom is to threaten the social order itself.

memes.label: Dankrat
memes.description: Dankrat is the deceased former king of Burgundy, husband of Ute, and father of Gunther, Gernot, Geiselher, and Kriemhild.

memes.label: Dankwart
memes.description: Dankwart is the brother of Hagen of Tronje and the marshal of the Burgundian court — a fierce warrior who kills Bloedel, Etzel's brother, in the final battle, thereby triggering the all-out war that destroys both sides.

memes.label: Danube River
memes.description: The Danube River in the Nibelungenlied is the major geographical boundary crossed by the Burgundians on their journey to Etzel's court — the site of Hagen's encounter with the water nymphs, the killing of the ferryman, and the prophetic knowledge that none will return from Hunnish lands.

memes.label: The Danube Crossing
memes.description: The Burgundians' crossing of the Danube into Hunland — a passage marked by Hagen's killing of the ferryman, the water nymphs' dire prophecy that only the chaplain will return, and Hagen's ritual sacrifice of the chaplain to test fate, confirming that the journey is a death march.

memes.label: Danube Ford
memes.description: The Danube Ford is the river crossing point in Bavaria where Hagen encounters the water nymphs, kills the ferryman Else, and leads the Burgundian army across the Danube — the point of no return that Hagen immediately recognizes as a fatal boundary.

memes.label: Death in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Death (Tod) as the structural and thematic endpoint of the Nibelungenlied — from the programmatic foreshadowing of the opening stanzas to the universal annihilation of the final adventure.

memes.label: Dietrich von Bern
memes.description: Dietrich von Bern (Theodoric of Verona) is the exiled King of the Goths living at Etzel's court — the epic's most formidable warrior who refuses to fight in Kriemhild's revenge until directly attacked, then subdues both Hagen and Gunther, only to weep over the dead alongside Etzel at the poem's close.

memes.label: Dwarf-Folk
memes.description: The dwarf-folk (Gezwerge) as subterranean beings in the Nibelungenlied — guardians of the Nibelung treasure, possessors of magical artifacts, and vassals of Siegfried after his conquest of the Nibelung realm.

memes.label: Eckewart
memes.description: Eckewart, a Burgundian margrave who serves as Kriemhild's chamberlain and guardian, accompanying her to Hunland and warning the Burgundians of danger upon their arrival at Etzel's court.

memes.label: Else
memes.description: Else is a Bavarian margrave who, together with his brother Gelfrat, rules the Bavarian March and opposes the Burgundian army crossing the Danube.

memes.label: Entertainment and Festivity in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Spiel (game, contest, music) and Kurzweil (pastime, entertainment) as the fabric of courtly life in the Nibelungenlied — the games, tournaments, minstrel performances, and festivities that define civilized existence and whose progressive disappearance marks the poem's descent into violence.

memes.label: Êre
memes.description: Êre (honor/glory) is the supreme social value in the Nibelungenlied — the public reputation and renown that motivates heroic action, governs courtly behavior, and, when wounded, demands vindication through vengeance.

memes.label: Etzel
memes.description: Etzel (Attila) is the King of the Huns and Kriemhild's second husband — a powerful but essentially passive monarch who provides the arena for Kriemhild's revenge and is reduced to helpless grief as his kingdom and court are destroyed around him.

memes.label: Etzel's Court
memes.description: Etzel's Court is the magnificent royal seat of King Etzel in the land of the Huns (Heunenland), the primary setting of the Nibelungenlied's second part — the site of Kriemhild's vengeance and the catastrophic destruction of the Burgundian host.

memes.label: Falcon Dream
memes.description: The falcon dream is Kriemhild's prophetic vision in the opening adventure of the Nibelungenlied — she dreams of rearing a wild falcon, strong and beautiful, torn apart by two eagles, a symbol that her mother interprets as foretelling the death of a future husband.

memes.label: Fall of the Burgundians
memes.description: The fall of the Burgundians is the apocalyptic finale of the Nibelungenlied — Kriemhild's methodical destruction of her own kin at Etzel's court, where the flower of Burgundian knighthood perishes in a burning hall, and the Nibelung treasure's location dies with Hagen.

memes.label: Fate and Doom
memes.description: Fate and doom are pervasive themes in the Nibelungenlied — the sense of inevitable destruction that hangs over the narrative from its opening stanzas, manifesting in prophetic dreams, the death of heroes, and the final conflagration at Etzel's hall.

memes.label: Feast and Festival
memes.description: The courtly feast (Hochzeit/hôchgezît) as a structuring narrative device in the Nibelungenlied — sites of political alliance, status display, gift exchange, and catastrophic reversal.

memes.label: Feudal Bond
memes.description: The feudal bond (Lehn) in the Nibelungenlied is the reciprocal relationship between lord and vassal — the grant of land and protection in exchange for military service and loyalty — that structures the political world of Burgundy and shapes the obligations driving the epic's conflicts.

memes.label: Fire and Burning in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Fire (Feuer) and burning (Brand) as a narrative and symbolic force in the Nibelungenlied — from the sparks of tournament combat to the conflagration that consumes Etzel's hall, fire marks the poem's movement from celebration to annihilation.

memes.label: Forest and Wilderness
memes.description: Forest and wilderness (Wald) in the Nibelungenlied as a space beyond courtly order — the site of hunting, ambush, concealment, and the encounter between human civilization and untamed nature.

memes.label: Friendship and Companionship in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Freund (friend, kinsman, ally) as the Nibelungenlied's most complex relational term — a word that encompasses kinship, political alliance, personal loyalty, and martial comradeship, and whose systematic betrayal drives the poem's catastrophe.

memes.label: Gaze and Spectacle in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: The window (Fenster) as a narrative frame and the act of looking (Schauen) as a social mechanism in the Nibelungenlied — how the courtly gaze structures gender relations, knightly performance, and the poem's visual economy.

memes.label: Geiselher
memes.description: Geiselher is the youngest of the three Burgundian brothers and co-kings — the kindest of the royal siblings, betrothed to Rüdiger's daughter, who fights and dies at Etzel's court as the sole Burgundian whose death is explicitly mourned by the opposing side.

memes.label: Gelfrat
memes.description: Gelfrat is a Bavarian margrave who, together with his brother Else, controls the Danube crossing and opposes the Burgundian army on their journey to Hunland, dying in the ensuing skirmish.

memes.label: Gender and Femininity in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Gender roles and the construction of femininity in the Nibelungenlied — how the poem represents women (Frauen) as objects of desire, agents of destruction, and bearers of the poem's emotional and ethical complexity.

memes.label: Generosity and Liberality
memes.description: Generosity (milte/milde) as a cardinal courtly virtue in the Nibelungenlied — the open-handed distribution of gifts and favor that defines noble identity, establishes political alliances, and is systematically undone by the poem's movement toward destruction.

memes.label: Gere
memes.description: Gere, a Burgundian margrave, marshal, and diplomatic envoy at the court of Worms, cousin to Kriemhild and companion of Eckewart.

memes.label: Gernot
memes.description: Gernot is the middle Burgundian brother and co-king — a capable warrior and diplomat who shares the rule of Burgundy with Gunther and Geiselher, and who dies fighting Rüdiger at Etzel's court in a mutual slaughter that epitomizes the epic's tragedy.

memes.label: Gift-Giving
memes.description: Gift-giving (Gabe/Milte) as the central mechanism of social bonding, political alliance, and status display in the Nibelungenlied's courtly world.

memes.label: Gotelind
memes.description: 'Wife of Rüdiger von Bechelaren, gracious hostess to Kriemhild on her journey to the Hunnish court, and a model of courtly hospitality in the epic'

memes.label: Gratitude and Reward in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Dank (gratitude, thanks) and Lohn (reward, recompense) as the twin mechanisms of reciprocity in the Nibelungenlied — the verbal and material currencies through which service is acknowledged, loyalty is secured, and the poem's economy of obligation is maintained until it collapses into vengeance.

memes.label: Greeting and Salutation in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: The greeting (Gruß) as a ritual of social recognition and power in the Nibelungenlied — how the giving, withholding, and interpretation of formal salutations structure the poem's social world and mark its moments of gravest crisis.

memes.label: Gunther
memes.description: Gunther is the king of Burgundy in the Nibelungenlied — the eldest of three royal brothers, Siegfried's brother-in-law and liege lord, whose inability to balance his obligations as king, brother, and husband sets the conditions for Siegfried's murder and the subsequent destruction of his entire court.

memes.label: Hagen
memes.description: Hagen of Tronje is the central antagonist of the Nibelungenlied — Gunther's most loyal vassal, the murderer of Siegfried, the thief of the Nibelung treasure, and the defiant warrior whose refusal to submit to Kriemhild drives the epic's catastrophic finale.

memes.label: Hagen's Oath
memes.description: The oath sworn by Hagen and Gunther never to reveal the location of the Nibelung treasure sunk in the Rhine — an act of defiant secrecy that becomes the epic's final mystery, carried to the grave by Hagen, and the last leverage he holds against Kriemhild.

memes.label: The Hall Burning
memes.description: Kriemhild's order to set Etzel's hall ablaze with the Burgundians trapped inside — a desperate escalation that turns the hall battle into an inferno, forcing the Burgundians to drink the blood of the fallen to survive the flames.

memes.label: Hawart
memes.description: Hawart, a Danish hero and lord who arrives at Etzel's court for Kriemhild's wedding and fights alongside Iring in the final battle, where he and his thousand men are slain by the Burgundians.

memes.label: Heathen and Pagan in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: The Christian–heathen (Christen–Heiden) distinction in the Nibelungenlied — how the poem navigates the religious divide between the Christian Burgundian world and Etzel's Hunnish court, and what this reveals about medieval attitudes toward religious difference.

memes.label: Helche
memes.description: First wife of Etzel (Attila the Hun), deceased before the events of the Nibelungenlied.

memes.label: Heroic Code
memes.description: The heroic code in the Nibelungenlied is the unwritten system of values — courage (Muth), strength (Kraft), boldness (Kühnheit), and the pursuit of renown — that governs the behavior of warriors and defines the ideal of the Recke (heroic knight).

memes.label: Hildebrand
memes.description: 'Master-at-arms and loyal retainer of Dietrich von Bern, veteran warrior who plays a pivotal role in the final battle at the hall of Etzel'

memes.label: Horses and Mounts in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Horses (Ross, Pferd) as instruments of mobility, gifts of lordship, and markers of prestige in the Nibelungenlied — the poem's most frequently mentioned material possession after clothing and weapons.

memes.label: Hospitality and Guest-Right
memes.description: The sacred obligation of hospitality (Gastlichkeit) and its catastrophic violation in the Nibelungenlied — from the courtly welcome at Worms to the fatal inversion at Etzel's hall.

memes.label: Huld — Grace and Favor in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Huld — the grace, favor, and benevolent disposition that structures political and personal relationships in the Nibelungenlied — a sovereign virtue whose withdrawal (Huldverlust) precipitates the poem's crises and whose absence marks the final catastrophe.

memes.label: Hun Court
memes.description: The Hun Court is the royal court of King Etzel (Attila the Hun) in the Nibelungenlied — the vast and powerful eastern kingdom where Kriemhild finds refuge after Siegfried's murder, and the stage for the epic's catastrophic finale in which the entire Burgundian court is annihilated.

memes.label: Characters of the Hun Court
memes.description: A comprehensive listing of all characters associated with King Etzel's Hun Court in the Nibelungenlied, the epic's second great political center and the stage for its catastrophic conclusion.

memes.label: Hunland
memes.description: Hunland (Heunenland) is the eastern kingdom ruled by King Etzel in the Nibelungenlied — the destination of Kriemhild's marriage journey and the setting of the epic's catastrophic finale, where the Burgundians meet their doom at Etzel's court.

memes.label: Hunold
memes.description: Hunold serves as chamberlain (Kammerer) at the Burgundian court, responsible for managing the royal household and ceremonial arrangements.

memes.label: Hunting in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: The hunt (Jagd) in the Nibelungenlied — the courtly ritual that becomes the setting for Siegfried's murder, transforming a celebration of aristocratic prowess into an act of treacherous violence.

memes.label: Iring
memes.description: 'Margrave of Denmark and vassal of Etzel, a valiant warrior who fights Hagen and the Burgundian kings in single combat during the hall-battle, wounding Hagen before falling himself'

memes.label: Irnfried
memes.description: Irnfried, a hero from Thuringia who fights alongside the Burgundians and is slain during the final battle at Etzel's court.

memes.label: Isenstein
memes.description: Isenstein is Brunhild's island fortress-kingdom in the Nibelungenlied — the remote stronghold where the warrior-queen sets her deadly contests for suitors and where Gunther, aided by Siegfried, wins her as his bride.

memes.label: Journey and Travel in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Journey (Reise, Fahrt) as the primary narrative engine of the Nibelungenlied — every major plot development is precipitated by a journey, and the poem's tragic arc is structured as a sequence of fateful arrivals and departures.

memes.label: Joy and Celebration in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Joy (Freude, Wonne) as the emotional keynote of courtly life in the Nibelungenlied — the celebration that defines the poem's first half and is systematically destroyed in its second, tracing the arc from "Freuden" to "Leid."

memes.label: Knighthood and Chivalric Identity in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Ritterschaft (knighthood, chivalry) as the social framework and shared identity of the Nibelungenlied's warrior elite — the poem's characters define themselves through knightly service, measure each other by chivalric standards, and invoke Ritterschaft as both a source of honor and a constraint on conduct, even as the narrative exposes the limits of chivalric ideology in the face of revenge and political necessity.

memes.label: Kriemhild
memes.description: Kriemhild is the central female figure of the Nibelungenlied — a Burgundian princess who transforms from a beautiful maiden untouched by love into a grieving widow and ultimately into a methodical avenger whose quest for vengeance for Siegfried's murder destroys the Burgundian court at Etzel's hall.

memes.label: Kriemhild's Death
memes.description: Kriemhild's death at the hands of Hildebrand — the final act of the Nibelungenlied in which Kriemhild, having beheaded the bound Hagen, is cut down by Dietrich von Bern's aged master-at-arms, who cannot bear to see a knight killed by a woman, ending the epic in a stark tableau of corpses.

memes.label: Kriemhild's Marriage to Etzel
memes.description: Kriemhild's marriage to King Etzel of the Huns — the political union brokered by Margrave Rüdiger that transforms Kriemhild from a grieving widow into a powerful queen and provides her with the resources to pursue her revenge against Hagen and the Burgundians.

memes.label: Lamentation and Mourning
memes.description: The ritual expression of grief through weeping and lament (Klage) — a pervasive emotional register of the Nibelungenlied that structures public and private responses to death.

memes.label: Laughter and Mockery in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Laughter (Lachen) and mockery (Spott) as instruments of social regulation and narrative provocation in the Nibelungenlied — how public ridicule triggers the poem's central crises and drives its tragic momentum.

memes.label: Lindger and Lindgast
memes.description: Saxon and Danish kings who invaded Burgundy in Adventure 4, decisively defeated by Siegfried and his forces.

memes.label: Lochheim
memes.description: Lochheim is the location on the Rhine where Hagen sinks the Nibelung treasure in Adventure 19, depriving Kriemhild of her inheritance and transforming the hoard from a material object into a legendary lost wealth — one of the epic's most consequential acts.

memes.label: Mercy and Compassion
memes.description: Mercy (erbarmen/Gnade) as a contested ethical category in the Nibelungenlied — repeatedly invoked, occasionally granted, and ultimately denied in the poem's movement toward total destruction.

memes.label: Messenger and News-Bearing in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: The messenger (Bote) and the transmission of news (Mär, Botschaft) as the narrative mechanism that links the Nibelungenlied's geographically dispersed courts — from the Saxon declaration of war to the fatal invitation that brings the Burgundians to Etzel's hall.

memes.label: Minne
memes.description: Minne is the Middle High German concept of courtly love central to the Nibelungenlied — an aristocratic ideal of romantic devotion that drives key plot developments including Siegfried's quest for Kriemhild and the rivalry between the two queens.

memes.label: Misfortune and Ill-Fate
memes.description: The vocabulary of misfortune (Übel) and affliction (Ungemach) as a pervasive emotional and thematic register in the Nibelungenlied — marking the poem's movement from courtly celebration to catastrophic destruction.

memes.label: Netherlands
memes.description: The Netherlands (Niederlande) in the Nibelungenlied is the kingdom ruled by Siegmund and Sieglind — the Lower Rhine realm where Siegfried grows up as a prince, from which he departs for Burgundy, and where he returns in death to be buried at Xanten.

memes.label: Nibelung and Schilbung
memes.description: The dwarf brothers Nibelung and Schilbung, original owners of the Nibelung treasure and rulers of Nibelungenland, slain by Siegfried in Adventure 3.

memes.label: Nibelung Castle
memes.description: The Nibelung Castle is the mountain stronghold where the Nibelung treasure was kept, guarded by the dwarf Alberich — conquered by Siegfried from Kings Schilbung and Nibelung, and the source of his legendary wealth including the sword Balmung and the Tarnkappe.

memes.label: Nibelung Treasure
memes.description: The legendary hoard of the Nibelungs — a vast treasure of red gold won by Siegfried, claimed by Kriemhild, and ultimately sunk in the Rhine by Hagen, serving as the central object of conflict throughout the Nibelungenlied.

memes.label: Nibelungen
memes.description: A mythical kingdom and people ruled by kings Schilbung and Nibelung, later conquered by Siegfried, guardians of the legendary Nibelungenhort.

memes.label: Nibelungenland
memes.description: Nibelungenland is the mythical kingdom of the Nibelungs in the Nibelungenlied — the subterranean realm rich in red gold, conquered by Siegfried, and the source of the Nibelung treasure, the sword Balmung, and the Tarnkappe.

memes.label: Oath
memes.description: The oath (Eid) in the Nibelungenlied is the binding verbal contract that creates obligations between individuals — the mechanism by which Alberich swears fealty to Siegfried, warriors pledge mutual defense, and, when violated, the breach that demands vengeance.

memes.label: Odenwald
memes.description: The Odenwald is the forest where Siegfried is murdered by Hagen during a hunting expedition — the pivotal location of the Nibelungenlied's central tragedy, where the heroic world of the first half of the epic collapses into the revenge-driven violence of the second half.

memes.label: Ortlieb
memes.description: 'The infant son of Etzel and Kriemhild, baptised into the Christian faith, whose brutal murder by Hagen in Adventure 33 ignites the final slaughter at the Hunnish court'

memes.label: Peace and Reconciliation in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Friede (peace) and Sühne (reconciliation) as a central tension in the Nibelungenlied — constantly invoked, promised, and sworn, yet systematically broken as the poem moves from the fragile peace of the Burgundian court to the annihilation of the final adventure.

memes.label: Pentecost Festival at Worms
memes.description: The Pentecost festival at Worms where Siegfried first sees Kriemhild — the emotional high point of Part I and the moment that initiates the epic's central love story, set against the splendor of Gunther's court.

memes.label: Pilgrim of Passau
memes.description: Bishop of Passau and uncle of the Burgundian royal family, who appears in the later adventures of the Nibelungenlied.

relations.label: Church and Religion in the Nibelungenlied

memes.label: Power and Dominion in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Gewalt (power, force, dominion) as the organizing principle of political relations in the Nibelungenlied — from Siegfried's claim that no one can win Kriemhild "mit Gewalt" to the poem's final demonstration that all courtly order rests ultimately on the capacity for violent coercion, the poem anatomizes power in its personal, political, and catastrophic dimensions.

memes.label: Quarrel of the Queens
memes.description: The quarrel of the queens at the church door is the pivotal confrontation of the Nibelungenlied — Kriemhild and Brunhild dispute precedence, leading Kriemhild to reveal the truth of Brunhild's deception, publicly humiliating the queen of Burgundy and setting in motion the chain of events that leads to Siegfried's murder.

memes.label: Revenge
memes.description: Revenge (Rache) is the primary narrative engine of the Nibelungenlied's second half — Kriemhild's methodical vengeance for Siegfried's murder drives the epic from tragedy to cataclysm, culminating in the destruction of the Burgundian court at Etzel's hall.

memes.label: Rhine
memes.description: The Rhine River in the Nibelungenlied is both a geographical setting and a symbolic space — the artery of the Burgundian world along which courts are seated, heroes travel, and the Nibelung treasure is ultimately sunk, never to be recovered.

memes.label: Rüdiger
memes.description: Rüdiger is the Margrave of Bechelaren — Etzel's most loyal vassal, Kriemhild's escort and oath-bound protector, Geiselher's future father-in-law, and the most tragic figure in the epic, forced by conflicting oaths to attack the very man who will soon be his son-in-law, dying in mutual slaughter with Gernot.

memes.label: Rüdiger's Dilemma
memes.description: Rüdiger's tragic dilemma in Adventure 37 — caught between his oath to Kriemhild and his friendship with the Burgundians, the Margrave of Bechelaren must choose whom to fight, and the choice destroys him as utterly as those he is forced to kill.

memes.label: Rumold
memes.description: Rumold, the kitchen-master of the Burgundian court at Worms, whose famous counsel against the journey to Hunland provides the epic's most memorable voice of prudence and foreshadows the catastrophe to come.

memes.label: Safe-Conduct and Protection in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Geleit (safe-conduct, escort, protection) as the social mechanism that guarantees safe passage through hostile territory in the Nibelungenlied — the ritual of escort that binds guest to host in an obligation of protection, whose violation marks the collapse of the poem's political order.

memes.label: Saxon Invasion
memes.description: The Saxon invasion is Siegfried's first great service to the Burgundian court — when Saxon and Danish armies threaten Burgundy, Siegfried leads the counterattack, capturing the enemy kings and cementing his status as the indispensable hero of Gunther's realm.

memes.label: Saxony and Denmark
memes.description: Saxony and Denmark are the northern kingdoms ruled by Liudeger and Liudegast in the Nibelungenlied — the enemies of Burgundy whose invasion is repelled by Siegfried in Adventure 4, establishing his reputation as the greatest warrior of his age.

memes.label: Service and Fealty in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Dienst (service, fealty) as the foundational social bond in the Nibelungenlied — the voluntary offering of loyalty and labor that structures relationships between lords and vassals, knights and ladies, and ultimately determines who lives and who dies when service obligations conflict.

memes.label: Shame and Disgrace in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Shame (Schande) as the negative pole of the Nibelungenlied's honor system — the disgrace that must be avoided at all costs, the accusation that drives the poem's central conflicts, and the social death that is feared more than physical death.

memes.label: Siegfried
memes.description: Siegfried is the central hero of the Nibelungenlied — the prince of Xanten and king of Nibelungenland, a warrior of superhuman strength who wins Kriemhild through his service to Gunther, only to be murdered by Hagen, setting the epic's revenge narrative in motion.

memes.label: Siegfried's Funeral
memes.description: Siegfried's funeral at Worms — the solemn burial of the murdered hero marked by Kriemhild's vigil at the bier, the cruentation miracle in which Hagen's approach causes Siegfried's wounds to bleed anew, and the lavish burial at the cathedral that establishes the martyr-like status of the slain hero.

memes.label: Siegfried's Murder
memes.description: Siegfried's murder is the central turning point of the Nibelungenlied — during a hunting expedition in the Vosges, Hagen spears Siegfried through his one vulnerable spot as he drinks from a spring, an act of treacherous violence that transforms the epic from heroic narrative into revenge tragedy.

memes.label: Sieglind
memes.description: Sieglind, Queen of the Netherlands, wife of King Siegmund and mother of Siegfried, renowned for her nobility and virtue.

memes.label: Siegmund
memes.description: Siegmund is the King of the Netherlands (Niderland), father of Siegfried, and husband of Queen Sieglind — a loving father who survives his son's murder and attempts to avenge him before being constrained by feudal loyalty.

memes.label: Sindold
memes.description: Sindold serves as cupbearer (Schenke) at the Burgundian court under King Gunther, responsible for the royal wine service and ceremonial drink distribution.

memes.label: Sorrow and Suffering in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Sorrow and suffering (Leid) as the Nibelungenlied's declared subject — from the opening stanza's programmatic "Liebe mit Leide" to the universal lamentation of the final adventures.

memes.label: Superhuman Strength
memes.description: Superhuman strength is a defining motif of the Nibelungenlied — from Siegfried's legendary might that conquers kingdoms to Brunhild's peerless physical power that must be overcome by deception, extraordinary strength marks the epic's heroes as beings who transcend ordinary human limits.

memes.label: Tarnkappe
memes.description: The Tarnkappe is a magical cloak of invisibility in the Nibelungenlied, won by Siegfried from the dwarf Alberich alongside the Nibelung treasure — it grants its wearer not only invisibility but also the strength of twelve men, playing a decisive role in Siegfried's deception of Brunhild.

memes.label: The Foreign and the Strange in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Fremde (the foreign, the strange) as a structural category in the Nibelungenlied — the poem's vocabulary for marking the boundary between native and foreign, guest and intruder, and the social obligations that govern encounters with those who come from outside.

memes.label: The Marvelous and Supernatural in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Wunder (marvels, wonders) as the narrative framing device and supernatural infrastructure of the Nibelungenlied — from the poem's opening promise of "viel Wunderdinge" to Siegfried's superhuman feats, the poem anchors its heroic world in the marvelous while situating wonders within a recognizably courtly and Christian framework.

memes.label: The Nibelungs in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: The Nibelungs (Nibelunge/Nibelungen) as the poem's most capacious and shifting identity marker — denoting a legendary dynasty, a subterranean people, the possessors of a cursed treasure, and ultimately the Burgundians themselves, the name "Nibelung" migrates across the poem, tracing a path from Siegfried's conquests to the poem's own title, and in its migrations reveals the poem's deepest logic: identity is possession, and to possess the Nibelung treasure is to become a Nibelung, with all the doom that name carries.

memes.label: Transport of the Nibelung Treasure
memes.description: The transport of the Nibelung treasure from Nibelungenland to Worms — Kriemhild's inheritance brought from the dwarf kingdom, a vast hoard that Hagen will later seize, and whose hidden location becomes the epic's final secret carried to the grave.

memes.label: Triuwe
memes.description: Triuwe (loyalty/fidelity) is a foundational virtue in the Nibelungenlied — the binding force of feudal relationships, sworn oaths, and personal devotion that stands in tension with the epic's narrative of betrayal and revenge.

memes.label: Tronje
memes.description: Tronje (also Tronege) is the ancestral seat of Hagen and his brother Dankwart in the Nibelungenlied — the stronghold from which Hagen derives his epithet "of Tronje" and which identifies him as a vassal lord of the Burgundian kings.

memes.label: Tuln
memes.description: Tuln is a Danube town in the Nibelungenlied where Kriemhild pauses on her journey to Etzel's court — a waystation between Bechelaren and Vienna that marks her passage from the Burgundian world into the Hunnic realm, where she first encounters the foreign customs of Etzel's people.

memes.label: Ute
memes.description: Ute is the Queen Mother of Burgundy — widow of King Dankrat, mother of Gunther, Gernot, Geiselher, and Kriemhild, and the interpreter of Kriemhild's prophetic falcon dream in the epic's opening.

memes.label: Virtue and Excellence in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Tugend (virtue, excellence) as the sum of knightly qualities in the Nibelungenlied — the poem's vocabulary for evaluating its heroes and marking the distance between the ideal (Rüdiger, "der Vater aller Tugend") and the catastrophic failure of virtue in the final act.

memes.label: Volker
memes.description: Volker von Alzey is the knight-minstrel of Burgundy — a warrior who plays the fiddle, a musician who wields a sword, and Hagen's inseparable comrade-in-arms at Etzel's court whose defiant music fills the final battle with epic grandeur.

memes.label: Vosges
memes.description: The Vosges Mountains (Wasgenwald in Middle High German) are the forested highlands east of the Rhine where Siegfried is treacherously murdered by Hagen during a hunting expedition in Adventure 16, marking the epic's central turning point.

memes.label: Wealth and Gold in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Gold (Gold) and treasure (Schatz, Hort) as the material engine of the Nibelungenlied's tragic plot — from bridal gift and feudal largesse to the cursed hoard that destroys kingdoms.

memes.label: Weapons and Armor in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Weapons and armor (Waffen, Schwert, Harnisch) as instruments of combat, objects of gift-exchange, and bearers of legendary identity in the Nibelungenlied — the material culture of the heroic warrior aristocracy.

memes.label: Wedding and Marriage in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Hochzeit (wedding, marriage feast) as the Nibelungenlied's central social ritual — the ceremony that creates alliances, transfers women between kingdoms, and stages the poem's most spectacular displays of power, hospitality, and destruction.

memes.label: Werbel and Swemmel
memes.description: Werbel and Swemmel, King Etzel's minstrels and envoys who deliver the fateful invitation to the Burgundian court, triggering the journey to Hunland.

memes.label: Wolfbrand
memes.description: Wolfbrand is one of Dietrich von Bern's warriors at Etzel's court, who fights in the final battle and falls in the great hall slaughter.

memes.label: Wolfhart
memes.description: 'Fierce young warrior of the retinue of Dietrich von Bern, nephew of Hildebrand, killed by his uncle in the climactic slaughter of Adventure 38'

memes.label: Worms
memes.description: Worms is the capital of the Burgundian kingdom on the Rhine, the primary setting of the Nibelungenlied's first part, where the Burgundian court receives Siegfried, plans the journey to Isenstein, and the tragedy of Siegfried's murder unfolds.

memes.label: Wrath and Hatred in the Nibelungenlied
memes.description: Wrath (Zorn) and hatred (Haß) as the affective engine of the Nibelungenlied's tragic arc — from Siegfried's battle-rage and Brunhild's bridal fury to Kriemhild's consuming hatred of Hagen, the passion that transforms a courtly world into a charnel house.

memes.label: Xanten
memes.description: Xanten is Siegfried's birthplace in the Lower Rhine region of the Netherlands (Niederlande), the city where he grows up as a prince and where he is later buried after his murder at the hands of Hagen.
